did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a computer-aided method involving recursive feature elimination (RFE) for feature selection and a support vector machine (SVM) classifier with a radial basis function (RBF) kernel to classify skin lesion types (melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and basal cell carcinoma) from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original integrated computer-aided method for skin lesion classification, detailing specific methods (morphological preprocessing, fractal-based feature extraction, RFE, SVM), experimental results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity metrics), and a conclusion based on new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Integration of morphological preprocessing and fractal based feature extraction with recursive feature elimination for skin lesion types classification
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of differentiating melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and basal cell carcinoma from dermoscopic images using an integrated computer-aided method. The proposed approach combines morphological preprocessing with fractal-based regional texture analysis (FRTA) and gray level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM) for feature extraction. Recursive feature elimination (RFE) is employed for feature selection, followed by a layered multiclass classification model using a support vector machine (SVM) with a radial basis function (RBF) kernel. The segmentation algorithm achieved a pixel-level sensitivity of 0.9172 and specificity of 0.9788, while the classification system attained accuracies of 98.99%, 97.54%, and 99.65% for melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and BCC, respectively. The results demonstrate that this combined quantitative and qualitative analysis significantly improves diagnostic accuracy over visual evaluation alone.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion types (melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and basal cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin diseases from dermoscopic images using a layered structure classification model with a support vector machine (SVM) classifier. The system identifies three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and basal cell carcinoma (BCC), achieving classification accuracies of 98.99%, 97.54%, and 99.65% respectively.

conclusions: The proposed integrated computer-aided system, which combines morphological preprocessing, fractal-based regional texture analysis (FRTA), gray level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM), and recursive feature elimination (RFE) with a support vector machine (SVM) classifier, effectively identifies melanoma, dysplastic nevi, and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from dermoscopic images.

The morphological skin lesion segmentation algorithm achieved a pixel-level sensitivity of 0.9172, specificity of 0.9788, accuracy of 0.9521, Jaccard similarity index of 0.8562, and Dice similarity coefficient of 0.9142.
The layered structure classification model achieved high classification accuracies of 98.99% for melanoma, 97.54% for dysplastic nevi, and 99.65% for BCC.

The combined approach of quantitative and qualitative analysis not only increases diagnostic accuracy compared to visual evaluation alone but also provides important information not obtainable from qualitative assessment alone.
important_remarks: The specific source, size, and composition of the dermoscopic image dataset are not reported, preventing assessment of population bias or dataset representativeness.
No external validation is performed; the study relies on a single, unspecified dataset, limiting generalizability.
Standard deviations or confidence intervals for the reported classification accuracies (98.99%, 97.54%, 99.65%) and segmentation metrics are not provided.
The paper does not report the number of images per class or the training/testing/validation split details, raising concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting.

The study compares the proposed method only against 'visual evaluation' qualitatively, lacking statistical comparison against other existing computer-aided diagnostic systems or inter-observer variability among dermatologists.
